reasoning_summary: The live run context is 20260421_152228_bf16_gemm_v1_2fbb368d at 24.392608 ms after swapping the default hot-band launch to the single-K 128x128 regular sibling. That edit produced a real but incomplete recovery: runtime improved by 0.144 ms versus the previous grouped-row regression, registers dropped from 200 to 196, and long-scoreboard stall improved from 7.33% to 7.15%. But the run is still 0.069087 ms slower than the clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4 at 24.323521 ms, and it remains 2.392608 ms behind the clean cuBLAS reference. So the kernel-variant swap moved the machine state in the right direction without solving the underlying latency-hiding wall. Because the user explicitly asked to widen family coverage from master heuristic history, the frontier was reseeded with several materially different families. The best next round is the lowest-risk exploit from that widened set: hoist invariant shared-offset math out of the active 128x128 steady-state loops so the current regular sibling keeps its small register win while shaving loop-carried control overhead. The second choice is a more aggressive PTX barrier-surgery family that returns to the accepted PTX surface without reintroducing the two-K-stage shared-memory blowup. The third choice is the high-ceiling 256x128 half-panel register-budget transplant, which stays queued because it is still the strongest way to attack the 16.6%-warps plateau, but it is too correctness-sensitive to auto-select ahead of the cheaper 128x128 exploit. The new persistent human guidance is now part of the search context as well. It explicitly biases the frontier toward 256x128 / 64x64 tiling, wide global-memory access, shared-memory reuse, async-copy pipelines, bank-conflict avoidance, L2 swizzle, register reuse, and deeper Pg2s/Ps2r/stage buffering. For this round that guidance is treated as a strong argument to keep the 256x128 transplant family and the PTX pipeline/barrier families hot in the widened frontier, even though the immediate recommendation still stays with the lower-risk 128x128 offset-hoist exploit on the current live surface.
notes: Round 3 explicitly folds master heuristic-history families into the ranking. The just-measured non-microkernel sibling is treated as partial positive signal, not a new base: it improved the previous regressed run but still did not reclaim the clean accepted runtime. Persistent human guidance from state/human_guidance.md is now part of every frontier-search read set.

family_audit.rationale: The current regular 128x128 sibling already recovered registers and scoreboard slightly, so the cheapest next step is a local hot-loop cleanup that preserves the same geometry and occupancy budget.
This round proved the regular sibling is directionally better than the grouped-row regression, but not strong enough to become the new accepted base.

The accepted clean base is still the PTX microkernel surface, and the vendor gap remains large enough that a targeted PTX barrier surgery still deserves an active slot. It also stays aligned with the human guidance around async copy, Pg2s/Ps2r overlap, and deeper staging without shared-memory blowup.

This remains the highest-ceiling family from master history because it is one of the only branches that materially changed occupancy, but it is too correctness-sensitive for the immediate next round. This family also aligns most directly with the new human guidance around 256x128 tiling, 64x64 warp tiles, and stronger register reuse.
The simpler 256x128 pivot promotion stays in frontier memory, but the more ambitious half-panel transplant has a better headroom case if we reopen 256x128 work at all.
The 64x384 row-band family is still a stale negative and does not match the current 128x128 latency-hiding evidence.

directions.name: Hoist 128x128 Hot-Band Shared Offsets Out Of The Steady-State Loop
directions.hypothesis: The regular 128x128 sibling already showed that a small register reduction and a cleaner consume path can recover some of the grouped-row loss. The next cheapest win is to hoist warp-local A-row and B-column shared offsets out of the hot-band steady-state loops so the active 128x128 path spends fewer issue slots on invariant pointer math while keeping the same CTA geometry, stage depth, and shared-memory footprint.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared-pointer arithmetic and loop-carried control overhead in the 128x128 hot-band steady state are still diluting tensor issue on a latency-limited path.
directions.risk: Low. The change stays inside the active 128x128 hot-band loops and should not alter CTA geometry, staging depth, or ownership.
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest follow-on after the regular-sibling experiment because it preserves the same recovered register footprint and tests whether control overhead is the remaining gap.
It also matches the user request to inject a materially different family without reopening a stale negative branch immediately.
This is the least aligned with the new 256x128 tiling preference, but it is still the cheapest next exploit on the current live 128x128 surface.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation needs to change CTA geometry, stage count, or dispatch order instead of remaining a local offset-hoist cleanup.
expected_local_changes: Precompute warp-local A and B shared offsets once per warp before the 128x128 steady-state loops.
Keep the current single-K staging and grouped_rows=4 traversal unchanged.

directions.name: Trim Microkernel Barriers Without Reintroducing Shared-Memory Blowup
directions.hypothesis: The accepted clean base is still the PTX microkernel surface, and the current regular sibling only recovered part of the loss. If the low-risk exploit is not enough, the next best family is to go back to the PTX single-K surface and trim wait/sync cadence more surgically while preserving the 22,016 B shared-memory footprint, instead of repeating the two-K-stage experiment that destroyed occupancy.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence inside the single-K 128x128 PTX microkernel is still interrupting the steady-state issue flow even on the smaller shared-memory footprint.
directions.risk: High. This is PTX-microkernel surgery on the accepted surface, so small schedule mistakes can erase the current stability or reintroduce barrier spikes.
ranking_notes: This keeps the highest-value PTX family alive without reopening the shared-memory blowup path that was already falsified.
It ranks behind dir_01 because the current live run suggests there is still cheaper control-overhead cleanup available on the active sibling.
This direction stays consistent with the human guidance on async copy, shared-memory reuse, and staging depth, but keeps the risk on the accepted PTX surface bounded.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory rises above the accepted 22,016 B footprint or if barrier stall does not improve materially versus the PTX accepted-base profile.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX microkernel launch surface if needed before editing the wait/sync cadence.
Retune the single-K consumer-barrier handoff without adding a second full K-tile shared buffer pair.

directions.name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
directions.hypothesis: If the 128x128 families stay trapped around 16.6% active warps, the highest-headroom branch left is still the 256x128 pivot family that historically proved the machine can run at far lower registers and much higher warps. The disciplined version is not to replay the broken branch, but to start from the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot ownership model and transplant only the low-register staging ideas that made the archived half-panel branch interesting.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and steady-state staging efficiency inside the 256x128 hot-band path are still capping residency and latency hiding on the current workload.
directions.risk: High. This is the highest-ceiling family left in the repo, but it touches the same 256x128 control/export surface that has failed correctness before.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
ranking_notes: This remains in the top three because it is one of the only branches in repo history that materially attacked the occupancy plateau instead of nudging stalls by tenths.
It ranks third because the correctness and implementation surface are both much larger than the two 128x128 options above it.
This is the direction that matches the new human guidance most directly because it pushes toward 256x128 tiling, stronger register reuse, and a deeper pipeline model.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if correctness fails again or if the edit revives the old split-writer ownership pattern.
expected_local_changes: Start from the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot launch surface.
Transplant only the low-register staging and reuse ideas from the archived half-panel branch, without reintroducing writer-split ownership.
